Back when the plandemic lockdowns initially began people were freaking out. There were several reasons for this. Obviously being afraid of getting sick or losing loved ones topped the list. That didn't concern me any more than it has at any other point. I know enough about germ theory to write it off as pseudoscience (see works like "good-bye germ theory" or research Pasteur in-depth). Frankly, I found the warnings and mandates from the so-called experts to be both insulting to the intelligence and downright laughable. As for the lockdowns themselves people were all bent out of shape about not being able to leave their homes. In the meantime I was thinking that no one needed to "order" me to stay home. As a recluse this is what I've always done and enjoy. I was social distancing long before it became the cool, virtue signaling thing for people to do! Already had a fully equipped gym and everything I needed, although I still went out whenever a need arose. Most people around me believed the "news", I laughed at it. Many people were already starting to wear face mask, I told them to read the disclaimer on the box they were getting them from, etc. To sum things up, I felt like one of the few remaining people capable of critical thought left in my area. I was calm while most everyone else was freaking out, and eventually managed to calm down several of those around me. I feel pretty good about that.

I went from a confident and successful business owner to being a self-conscious and defeated middle-aged man who had no clue how to move forward in life! I felt depressed and deflated.I also felt that I failed myself but mostly my family. It took me several years to recover and regain my self-worth. I had to go back to college and reinvent myself. The road was not easy but I made it. I earned a Bachelor of Computer Science degree and started working as a web developer. I'm currently on he path to building my own web and programming company and starting to feel like myself, once more.

S1_cl_reflect: A time when I felt unsure of who I was absolutely in college. Almost the whole time I felt that way on and off, but I remember one time in particular. For one of my classes in order to pass I had to create a portfolio and go to a job fair. This was all in the interest of developing me professionally. But to me it was coded language for making up who you are. I felt I had to pretend to embellish things about myself in order to be successful and meet the criteria for passing the class and essentially for succeeding in the "real world." I felt so confused and back and forth about it. I felt like I was doing things just because I thought I was supposed to, something I don't think myself as doing. I was conflicted. I was upset. I was uncomfortable and awkward to the max. I remember going up to the job fair and walking around it once avoiding eye contact with all of the booths and having to go to the bathroom to avoid a panic attack. I was so out of my element. After a while of calming down in the bathroom I really had to make a resolution with myself. That this wasn't me and I shouldn't do things that aren't me. So I left the job fair, I wasn't sure if it would hurt my grade because I didn't meet with anyone at the job fair, but I didn't care, I was tired of pretending to be a part of this "professional" world that I wasn't/

I have historically based who I am on what I do. In school, track, and band, I was highly focused on my performance as a measure for my worth and success. When I would chip a note on the French Horn, throw short or foul out in track (I participated in shot put and discus), or receive a poor grade on a test or assignment, I would come down pretty hard on myself. I would run the scenario over and over in my head and ask myself how I could have disappointed my teacher, coaches, directors, classmates, and family - even though they all understood perfectly well that mistakes happen and that my worth was not dependent on my performance. More recently, and most severely, the COVID-19 pandemic resulting in the closing of schools and the termination of extra curricular activities left me to feel anxious and depressed at the sudden lack of opportunity for me to excel as I was used to. Thankfully, this did provide opportunity for me to learn and grow as I continued to work past my perfectionism and into a reality with a focus on presence and welcoming of mistakes, rather than performance and anxiety of mistakes.

The one instance that comes to mind is when I was a teenager and first starting high school. The people who were surrounding me made me doubt my own views and how I viewed myself. I didn't really fit in with the crowd when going to school, and this caused a lot of internal conflict. I wondered whether I should stay true to what I was or whether I should try to adopt the perspectives, views and tastes of those around me. Of course, I tried branching out a bit in things that I liked to do, listen to, watch, etc to try to find common ground with those around me to try and make some friends. Of course it worked, because we're talking about teenagers here, but something inside of me never really clicked whenever I was trying to share interests with those around me. I always found myself drifting back to what I liked and what I wanted to do rather than what everyone else was doing. So my main point here is that I was juggling two personalities to try and please others and it made me feel like I wasn't being true to myself and I didn't know who I really was.

S1_cl_reflect: I think the time in my life where this prompt most applies was when I was in high school. When I was a teenager I was really torn about how I wanted to present myself and interact with others. Some days I felt as though I wanted to be cool, aloof, hard to know. I would purposefully avoid talking to others, generally reading a book or magazine at times when I wasn't in class. I was not friendly; I was very sarcastic and cynical. I would let people know when I disagreed with them, why I disagreed with them, and often why I thought they were not as intelligent as I was. All in all, a fairly unpleasant person. Other days, however, I felt an urgent need to belong. I wanted many friends, I wanted people to want to know me and talk to me. I felt especially awkward on these days because I also felt quite uncomfortable opening up to others. So it was like I wanted something that I didn't know how to obtain. I didn't know how to act to get other people to like me, and this caused a distinct rift in myself. Basically, I didn't know if I wanted to be an outsider or whether I wanted to belong.

S1_cl_reflect: I can recall a definite time that I felt I didn't have a clear concept of who I was and was in an inconsistent time of conflict. It was when I was having health issues and decided to do something about it. After doing a lot of research I decided to become a Vegan. During the first four to six months I felt better and thought I was doing good. I even changed my concept of eating animals and started taking on the Vegan way of thinking. I still didn't understand completely why I did that and there were conflicts on who I thought I was because of that. After a year I was far worst in bad medical conditions than when I started but I carried on thinking I was doing the right thing. After two years my health deteriorated to where I had to try something else. I knew about Dr. Atkins diet from being on it in the 1980s and read more about the keto diet. I went on that diet and within a few months I was better than I ever was for many decades. I am now on the Carnivore diet and it solved all my issues, cleared up my brain fog to where I now know who I am and have a clear sense of who I am.
S1_purpose: movie ratings based on ability to analyse by being able to under conflicted conditions.
